## Decision Making Frameworks
***3 Frameworks** - Six thinking hats, One-Way Vs. Two-Way Doors, Lindy Effect*
<empty-block/>
<callout icon="💡" color="gray_bg">
	Decision making is the process of choosing between two or more alternative courses of action. This intro callout belongs to the category, not to any single framework.
</callout>
<empty-block/>
### Six thinking hats
- Reference - [Article](https://www.debonogroup.com/services/core-programs/six-thinking-hats/)
- Author - Edward De-bono
- Overview -
	- The Six Thinking Hats framework is a decision-making technique that helps individuals and teams look at problems from a variety of perspectives.
	- Each hat represents a different mode of thinking:
		- **White Hat**: objective; focuses on facts and logic.
		- **Red Hat**: intuitive; focuses on emotion and instinct.
		![Image Credits - [https://www.figma.com/community/file/980483835848678492/six-thinking-hats](https://www.figma.com/community/file/980483835848678492/six-thinking-hats)](https://prod-files-secure.s3.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/593c798c/2da2bfb0/Untitled.png?X-Amz-Signature=deadbeef)
	- The technique encourages switching between hats to explore a problem from multiple angles.
<empty-block/>
### One-Way Vs. Two-Way Doors
- Reference - [Article](https://www.aboutamazon.com/news/company-news/2016-letter-to-shareholders)
- Author - Jeff Bezos
- Overview
	- The concept of one-way vs two-way door decisions categorizes decisions based on their level of reversibility and consequence.
		- **One-way door decisions**: consequential and irreversible or nearly irreversible.
		- **Two-way door decisions**: reversible and lower-consequence; can be made quickly.
<empty-block/>
<callout icon="💡" color="gray_bg">
	If you have not come across this concept, this is the most important decision making lens you are going to apply. Anytime you are stuck on a decision and you know its a two-way door just choose one and move on.
</callout>
<empty-block/>
### Lindy Effect
- Reference - [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lindy_effect), [Article](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/lindy-effect-gaurav-gupta/)
- Overview
	- The Lindy Effect suggests that the future life expectancy of non-perishable things, such as ideas, is proportional to their current age.
	- The longer something has existed, the more likely it is to continue to exist in the future.
<empty-block/>
<callout icon="💡" color="gray_bg">
	I personally use this as a barometer to find quality content. If people are referencing a piece after a decade, there is bound to be value contained.
</callout>
<empty-block/>
